data.title: ACWI: Core Global Equities Strategy
data.description:
The iShares MSCI ACWI ETF (ACWI) is a passively managed strategy designed to track large- and mid-cap companies in developed and emerging markets. The strategy can largely be utilized by passive investors seeking broad exposure to US and international equities, though it maintains similar characteristics to US indexes, exposing investors to the technology sector and mega-cap technology stocks.

About iShares MSCI ACWI ETF

ACWI was launched by iShares on March 26, 2008, on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has an expense ratio of 32 bps, aligned with peer global ETFs. ACWI pays out a modest distribution rate of $2.03/share, yielding 1.45% for the last twelve-month period. ACWI is broadly held with $24b in net assets and an average trading volume of $434mm in share value.

ACWI is benchmarked to the MSCI ACWI Index, which covers mid- and large-cap equities in developed and emerging markets. The index is a market capitalization-weighted index, meaning that US large-cap equities may be prioritized given the relative size of these constituents.

Accordingly, the Index is most heavily weighted towards the technology sector, accounting for 28.5% of the total Index.

ACWI is broadly diversified across 2,280 holdings, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 25% of net assets. Narrowing this down, the Magnificent 7 stocks account for 22.51% of net assets, suggesting significant concentration to the top holdings within the strategy. Though this doesn’t amount to the same level of concentration exhibited in the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), which sits at 48.26% for the Mag 7 stocks, I believe the concentration is still large with respect to the number of holdings within the portfolio. The large concentration in the top 10 holdings may result in these stocks having more influence over the direction of the total portfolio with respect to the remaining 75% of the holdings.

Thematically, I believe the technology sector has room for continued growth, particularly as the AI and cloud hosting theme remains intact following the Q3’26 earnings results by Nvidia (NVDA). On a global scale, I have reason to believe that the AI theme may be expanding across geographies beyond the US and China, driven by sovereign nations investing in localized infrastructure. This will likely drive further investment into companies tied to the broader market theme, including building infrastructure for constructing data centers, IT infrastructure for outfitting data centers, and power transmission and distribution for supplying power to data centers, reflecting the growth expected in the US over the coming years. Though investments will likely benefit US AI infrastructure companies like Nvidia most, given the technology advancements over the rest of the market, investing in developing localized data centers may provide benefits to local economies for the construction, energy, and industrials markets.

Investor Suitability

Net of the top technology holdings, ACWI is a well-diversified strategy that covers a broad range of holdings across each sector, providing minimal exposure to any single stock. ACWI can be utilized as a core investment, acting as the bedrock for building upon. I believe the best way to utilize ACWI is to build satellite positions on top of the core ACWI holding in order to gain exposure to underrepresented sectors that may appeal to the investor, such as energy or utilities.

ACWI can also be held as a placeholder as an equity position as investors seek out new investment opportunities, gaining beta growth in the meantime. For example, if an investor wishes to invest more heavily in the energy sector using an industry indexed strategy like the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), the investor could proportionally reduce exposure to ACWI while allocating to XLE.

At a high level, I do not believe ACWI will be an alpha-generating strategy given the broad diversification across constituents.

Fund Comparison

There are a number of passively managed core strategies investors may choose from to gain broad equity exposure, including the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (VT), Avantis All Equity Markets ETF (AVGE), or the Capital Group Global Equity ETF (CGGE), among others.

Though launched in 2024, CGGE has outperformed peer equity strategies on a 1-year, total return basis.

Comparing 10-year performance, which will not include CGGE or the Pacer MSCI World Industry Advantage ETF (GLBL), VT offers similar performance at a significantly lower fee of 6bps. Given the similar performance and exposure to global equities, VT may be a more appealing option given the low fee.

Risks Related to ACWI

ACWI is a broadly diversified, passively managed equity strategy that will provide investors with global exposure. Given the broad diversification, ACWI will mostly exhibit beta risk, or risk in association with the broader market. Investors must consider the significant concentration in US equities, the technology sector, and the mega-cap technology stocks held within the strategy, particularly when building a diversified portfolio around the ETF. Given the substantial concentration in these categories, US index exposure may exhibit substantial concentration risk, particularly in the mega-cap technology stocks.

Final Thoughts

ACWI can be an appealing core equity holding, providing investors with broad, global diversification across countries and industries. From an allocation perspective, ACWI can be utilized for gaining US and international equity exposure as a core holding. ACWI may also be used as an equity placeholder in between trades, providing beta growth as investors search for new investment ideas. Given the relative performance and fees, I am recommending investors consider VT as an alternative; however, given beta growth, I am recommending ACWI with a Buy rating.

data.title: Frontline: Rising Spot Rates Favoring Company's Cash Guidance Into 2026
data.description:
Shares of shipping company, Frontline plc (FRO), are up 32.62% (YoY) and 82.52% (YTD). The increase is despite Frontline’s Q3 2025 revenues declining 9.87% (QoQ), while net profits were down 48% (QoQ). I believe the increase is partly due to rising demand for tankers globally and growing shipments of crude oil, with buyers said to be exploring alternatives to Russian oil. With this in mind, I will explain why I’m rating this stock as a hold, coupled with the fact that it is trading at fair value.

Firstly, let’s explore the idea of growing tanker rates into 2026. Daily tanker rates have hit a 5-year high with supertankers said to be charging a historic rate of $137,000. In Q3 2025, Frontline attained a mean daily spot time charter equivalent (TCE) rate of $34,500 for VLCCs, $35,100 for Suezmax tankers and $31,400 for LR2/Aframax tankers. On sequential analysis, there was a 7.17% (QoQ) increase in the average TCE rates offered on chartering LR2/Aframax as compared to Frontline’s VLCCs and Suezmax tankers.

As noted earlier, on the Bloomberg report, there has been high tanker rates in the Middle East due to heightened crude oil production from OPEC+ producer states. The increased demand for crude oil from the Middle East (as an alternative to Russian oil) is set to continue forcing tanker rates to historic levels into 2026. By the end of October 2025, the daily average spot rates for VLCCs operating in the Middle East Gulf-China trade routes soared 42% (YoY) to $125,081.

This graph shows that into Q4 2025, the global daily average TCE levels (which at the time the graph was drawn stood at $108,833) were at levels last seen in April 2020. Reports further indicate that the MEG-East Coast India trade route registered a historic daily average TCE of $167,357.

Further, the larger vessel freight rates are rising across the board, affecting both oil/product tankers and dry bulk carriers. There has been a positive movement in the Baltic Dry Index (BDI) that shows the movement of raw materials (apart from oil) by sea.

The BDI stands at 2,295 points and is up 50.10% (YoY), showing the rising daily cost of shipping mineral items such as coal, iron ore and many more, which inherently means positing sea activity into 2026. For instance, dry bulk carrier Genco Shipping & Trading Limited is up 37.66% (YTD) and has risen 160% in the last 5 years. Another carrier, Star Bulk Carriers, has increased 32.11% (YTD) showing positive market sentiment into 2026. Further, FRO is dependent on the Baltic exchange rates in establishing its cash projections, as explained in its Q3 2025 earnings call, when it gave a cash guidance of $1.8 billion based on the Baltic Exchange recorded on November 18, 2025.

Order Book growth

Frontline expects to grow its order book into 2026 and 2027. As at Q3 2025, FRO’s tanker asset order book consisted of, and I quote “20.3% of the existing global fleet, with 135, 111, and 162 vessels on order for VLCCs, Suezmax tankers and LR2 tankers, respectively.” This is a significant number considering there are about 3 deliveries expected to be made in Q4 2025. As a replacement strategy, FRO is looking to maintain a consistent fleet growth at a CAGR of 15% (between 2025 to 2029). Then again, we have the introduction of eco-vessels, or a fleet of about 80 vessels that are energy efficient, with 56% of the fleet scrubber-fitted. We are looking at lower operational costs in 2026 since expenses took up more than 90% of FRO’s revenue, lowering earnings by $392.4 million. Additionally, FRO’s eco-vessels have an average age of 7.2 years, which is a young fleet considering Okeanis Eco Tankers, founded in 2018, has an environmentally compliant fleet with an average age of 4.6 years. At the onset, Frontline has a regulatory advantage as far as environmental compliance is concerned and is expecting higher demand from buyers and higher resale chances.

Overall, the crude oil tankers market is expected to reach $250 billion by 2032, from $180 billion in 2023, growing at a CAGR of 3.5% (between 2023-2025).

Valuation

I will use the price-to-earnings ratio to evaluate Frontline as it will incorporate the company’s net income against the share price. It will also help identify if the stock is potentially undervalued or overvalued, thereby determining how much the market can pay for the earnings into 2026.

FRO’s forward PE metric stands at 13.89X against the earnings per share (EPS) of 1.81. The computation of these two gives us the new share price of $25.14. As of this writing, FRO’s share price stood at $24.96 (a difference of +0.72%), meaning that the stock is trading at a fair valuation.

I will use the metric to compare the performance of FRO with some of its peers. We have to keep in mind that the PE sector median sits at 13.63X.

Sector Median (13.63X) Forward PE ratio Forward EPS Future Share price Current Share Price Valuation Dividend yield
FRO 13.89X $1.81 $25.14 $24.96 +0.72% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 3.04%
CMB.TECH NV. (CMBT) 46.14X $0.22 $10.15 $10.05 +1.0% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 0.49%
DHT Holdings, Inc. (DHT) 13.43X $0.99 $13.30 $13.06 +1.84% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 5.41%
Teekay Tankers Ltd. (TNK) 9.45X $6.45 $60.95 $59.48 +2.5% (YoY) trading at fair valuation 3.28%

The tabulated analysis shown above indicates that FRO and its peers are trading at their fair valuation, that is as far as their PE ratios are concerned. However, DHT is offering the highest annual dividend yield at 5.41% while CMBT has the lowest yield at 0.49%.

However, back to Frontline, the company stated that it had a cash generation potential of 30,000 on an annual basis, thereby giving guidance of $1.8 billion for the FY 2025. An increase at a range of 30% to 44.4% from the current average spot rate would raise the cash generated by the end of 2025 to $2.6 billion. It represents a cash growth of 519% (YoY) from $419.6 million realised in the FY 2024. FRO’s free cash flow as of December 2024 stood at $736.4 million. Therefore, an increase of 30% to 44.4% in FY 2025 will raise its FCF to the range of $957.32 million to $1.06 billion. Keeping in mind that FRO does not have any significant debts maturing before 2030, thereby allowing the company to retain more cash.

I would also like to consider FRO's cash breakeven rates into Q4 2025 and for the full year 2025.

On quarterly analysis, Frontline has surpassed its daily average cash breakeven rates into 2026 with the exception of it's Q1 2025 LR2/Aframax average daily spot TCE that fell 5.83% (YoY). While the numbers were stronger in 2024 (see table for comparison), all the average daily rates that is, $38,200 (VLCC), $35,100 (Suezmax) and $27,900 (LR2/Aframax) all exceeded the daily average cash breakeven rates for the 12 months of 2025. The main concern here is that, into Q4 2025; FRO had only covered 51% of its average daily TCE for LR2/Aframax which is equal to $21,522 indicating that it almost 10% short of the daily average cash breakeven rate of $23,600.

Risk

This valuation is dependent on the growth of tanker rates and positive Baltic Exchange rates that will ensure FRO realises higher earnings into 2026. A decline in the exchange rates and the spot TCEs will have an adverse effect on FRO’s valuation, as elucidated in this article.

Bottom Line

I have rated Frontline as a hold based on the rising shipments of crude oil away from Russia and growing tanker demand that has forced an increase in spot TCE rates. However, the stock is trading at a fair valuation but has the capacity to grow its free cash flow levels to $1.06 billion from a potential cash generation of $2.6 billion based on the company’s forecast of a 30% increase to the current spot rates into 2026.

data.title: Rates Spark: A Good Place For Rates And Sentiment
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Steady EUR rates and market sentiment with ECB in a good place, but risks of more easing likely understated

EUR rates are staying true to their ranges, even if testing out the upper regions of late, with the 10y swap rate just above 2.7%. Away from the ultra-long end, which had resumed its steepening at the start of this month, the curve also remains more range-bound. In fact, 2s10s is bang on the post-'Liberation Day' average.

Risk perception of broader markets has also returned to a more benign setting, with equities having recouped some of their recent losses. In rates markets, the implied volatilities have edged lower again, which in turn has helped bond spreads to re-tighten. Over the past few days, 10y Italian government bonds have marked their lowest spreads over Bunds since 2010. Even French bonds are seeing their tightest spreads since late August, when political turmoil began to first hit the market.

Markets could be interpreted as a reflection of the ECB's wait-and-see mode. The ECB minutes of the October meeting just reaffirmed that the central bank sees itself in a “good place.” On closer inspection, the ECB still holds a slight easing bias, and expectations of a gradual recovery are still surrounded by risks and uncertainties, such as around trade or the implementation of government stimulus and investment plans. Our economists also highlight the potential downward impact on inflation projections of the EU’s plans to delay the next phase of the emissions trading system.

On the back of this, we also think that short-end rates should move back to incorporate higher chances of more ECB easing in their pricing. For now, the market is looking at around a one-in-three chance of one more ECB cut over the course of next year. For the broader curve, that means a bias to re-steepen in our view. That also remains our longer-run view if the picture of a gradual recovery is confirmed and longer rates gradually move higher to test above the top of current ranges.

Friday’s events and market view

The US is still off, which should again make for quieter markets. Out of the eurozone, we will get the first country indications for the November CPI, with France, Spain, Italy and Germany reporting their preliminary figures. Overall, eurozone inflation is expected to stay little changed, with the consensus looking for a 2.1% YoY reading next week. Separately, the ECB will also release the results of its consumer survey on longer-run inflation expectations today, where the 1y horizon figure is anticipated to ease lower towards 2.6%.

No issuance is scheduled for today. In terms of scheduled rating reviews, S&P still has France on its calendar, but since it downgraded the rating to A+/Stable just last month, it is likely this data will be skipped.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Newegg Commerce: Fundamentals Do Not Justify The Valuation, Sell
data.description:
Introduction

Newegg Commerce (NEGG) has seen an amazing turnaround in its share price this past year, something that cannot be said about its financial situation. It seems the company is yet another meme stock that sees a lot of volatility, and I would recommend selling right now if you still own it, as the business model in the long term doesn’t seem viable without proper cost-cutting initiatives.

The Company

NEGG is an e-commerce company that sells PC hardware, consumer and enterprise electronics. Core offerings include desktops, laptops, PC components like GPUs and CPUs, motherboards, gaming peripherals, etc. It is a B2C and B2B platform.

The company makes its money through direct sales, so it buys up inventory and then resells it. It’s a rather straightforward business model.

Briefly on Financial Performance

Looking at NEGG’s sales performance over the last two years or so, we can see a downtrend that has been somewhat broken at the end of 2024, which is a good sign, but doesn’t necessarily tell us that everything is coming back up now. It is a good start. So far, the company has only reported its first half of the year performance, showing us that there has been a decent increase in sales of almost 13%, which was primarily driven by increased demand for GPUs, like Nvidia’s (NVDA) GeForce RTX 50 Series, and AMD’s (AMD) Radeon RX 9000 series GPUs and CPUs.

During the same time, GMV increased by around 13.7% to $849m, most likely driven by the same products as mentioned above. Average volume increased to $467 vs $401 in the same period last year, with repeat purchase rate increasing to 25.2% compared to 23% last year. Repeat purchaser is an active customer who has made at least two purchases on the website in the last six months.

Looking at the company’s efficiency and profitability, the company operates in a very tight margin business sector, and even with such an improvement in sales, the company is still not turning a profit; however, we can see that the first half of 2025 improved quite a bit due to the refreshed GPU and CPU sales. But even then, it doesn’t look like the company managed to turn a profit, which is concerning. Even the largest releases of the new GPUs and CPUs weren’t enough to help it turn green. EBITDA, which is a non-GAAP metric, is also negative.

If we adjusted the numbers further, which I am not a fan of, but most companies tend to do it to show a better picture, Adjusted EBITDA improved quite a bit from last year and turned positive, which is the only positive thing regarding its operations so far. We can see that stock-based compensation is one of the bigger items that affect the company.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation on its balance sheet. NEGG finished the quarter with around $59m in cash and equivalents against $15m in short-term debt, and $47m in lease liabilities. It’s worth noting that the company’s cash position decreased to $59m from $96m, and that is mainly due to the company’s inventory buildup. Compared to last year, the company’s inventory increased by a substantial amount, going from around $98.5m to $152m. Is it worrying? It is hard to tell right now, because the management is saying that it is “taking advantage of accelerated spending amid tariff uncertainties” and is working with partners to limit the tariff impact. So, in a way, they have an excuse to have the elevated inventory levels right now, fully expecting to sell through it, but it has to be monitored further.

Overall, the company’s recent financial performance doesn’t strike me as really good and definitely doesn’t justify such a share price appreciation. Additionally, the limited information for shareholders, with the lack of conference calls, is a bit disappointing. It is hard to get a decent picture of the company’s financials when it doesn’t provide a lot of information more often.

Comments on the Outlook

Another Meme Stock

As of right now, on Thanksgiving in the US, markets are closed, and so far this year, the company’s share price is up over 535%, sitting at around $76.5 per share. At one point this year, back in May, the company’s share price was as low as $3.32, which then skyrocketed to as high as $137.84. That’s a 4000% move from the lows in just two and a half months. Why the move? I remember browsing many financial subreddits and one of the bigger ones active right now, since the GameStop’s (GME) meme craze remains WallStreetBets. I remember seeing Newegg pop up quite a bit during the summer. Many financial media outlets during the same period labeled the company a new meme stock, noting that its run was driven primarily by social-media hype around the Gamer Community launch, the GPU buzz with NVDA. Being a rather lightly traded company, and at one point saw its short interest spike to over 300%, it was ripe for a meme stock short squeeze event.

In reality, all this news did very little to improve the company’s financial outlook. The company announced its full-year financial outlook, and the management expects sales to be in the range of $1.375B-$1.424B and still report a loss on the bottom line.

Since topping almost $138 a share on literally nothing but social-media hype, the company’s stock price began to fall. It fell to a low of $30 in just a couple of weeks, and over the last 2 months recovered quite a bit, keeping the meme craze alive.

The AI Hype story is helping

Sure, there were some real catalysts at the beginning of the year, like the refresh cycle of GPUs and CPUs, but that wasn’t enough to push its shares so high. NVDA is the AI darling in this growth story, and since NEGG is selling a lot of NVDA’s products, there will always be some sort of mentality to follow NVDA’s money. The new RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell GPUs that the management mentioned are going to help the second half of the year in terms of sales, are not going to drive efficiency higher, and since NVDA doesn’t release upgrades too often, typically, once every couple of years, which means the company is not going to get that boost in sales in the first half of 2026 at least. This will make its financial performance come in lower than what we saw this past half of the year.

The good thing about this AI growth story is that NVDA continues to see an amazing demand for its products regarding data centers and AI, with over 62% growth y/y primarily driven by Blackwell and Blackwell Ultra platforms, and with all the big tech still spending a ridiculous amount of cash on the AI race, companies like Newegg will most likely see some a lot of share price volatility going forward, especially if they continue to market NVDA products front and center, but it doesn’t seem like the business is going to get a lot of boost in the performance. It just looks like this retail business is not viable even during the good times, as it just cannot turn a profit on the biggest product launches of the year.

I want to circle back to the company’s inventory levels, which I think are more than just gearing up for a heavy season of buying. Sure, I can see some more sales coming through because the second half of the year is going to be full of holidays like Black Friday, Christmas, and some other holidays I’m sure I missed. Is the company going to sell through all of this, or is it a sign of things to come? I am going to eagerly await the next report to find out.

The most recent news regarding new developments with the company is that they announced a partnership with PayPal (PYPL) to offer an AI-powered shopping experience through portals like Perplexity. So, essentially agent AI service will allow customers to purchase PC components through an AI search engine. The news wasn’t big enough to make the company’s share surge, and I think this is not going to be such a big deal in the end, too.

Closing Comments

To sum up my feelings about the company, it doesn’t strike me as a fundamentally strong company with rational behavior regarding its share price. This is one of those companies that real long-term investors should avoid if they would like to preserve their wealth and not chase at the top. Sometimes it may be fun to plop down some cash that you got lying around but expect to lose most of it if you are chasing the momentum.

I expect to see further volatility because of AI and NVDA being a thing still, and because of the company’s reminiscence of gamer vibes comparable to GameStop, which will continue to bring upswings through the next meme craze, which seems to happen every month or so now.

The company’s business model doesn’t seem to support it at all, even when sales are through the roof. The company needs to find ways to cut costs and make it a lot more attractive fundamentally. It’s just not a viable business otherwise. I’m avoiding it, and I would recommend selling it while it is still very overvalued unless you don’t like money.

data.title: Janus Henderson Global Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

  • Global equities rose strongly in U.S. dollar terms on trade war de-escalation and signs of resilient economic growth in many countries.
  • U.S. market performance was especially strong, aided by expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts. The Fed reduced policy rates in September, with additional rate cuts expected by year-end. Stocks in Japan and the emerging markets also performed well, although European equities lagged after outperforming earlier in the year.
  • The market advance was relatively narrow, with notable gains in information technology stocks that rose on excitement around artificial intelligence (AI). Real estate was the weakest-performing market sector.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by a lack of exposure to several stocks that were very strong performers for the index. These included companies viewed as beneficiaries of AI. As investors have become more excited about the potential around the AI complex, they have driven shares of even indirect beneficiaries of AI-related spending to higher and higher valuations that we believe are unsupported by company fundamentals. While not owning these stocks detracted from relative performance, we remain steadfast in seeking to protect our investors from excessive risk, including valuation risk.

U.S.-based fast casual restaurant chain Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) was a notable detractor from relative performance. We originally invested in Chipotle because of its track record of operational efficiencies and digital leadership. In recent quarters, however, the company has faced business headwinds as cash-strapped consumers have cut back on spending at restaurants. As a result, the company reported two consecutive quarters of disappointing same-store sales growth, and it missed its earnings targets and reduced guidance. We have been disappointed with Chipotle’s recent execution, and we exited the position.

Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG) was another relative detractor, as insurance stocks have generally lagged in a market focused on higher growth investments. A global provider of insurance brokerage and risk management, Gallagher has taken advantage of industry fragmentation to grow its market share and strengthen its competitive position. The company has continued to deliver stable earnings growth and attractive returns on capital. We are also constructive on its accretive purchase of insurance broker AssuredPartners, which expands Gallagher's reach in the middle-market segment.

Contemporary Amperex Technology (OTC:CTATF) (CATL) was a top positive contributor to relative performance. This Chinese company controls more than 30% of the electric vehicle battery market, serving major manufacturers such as Tesla and Volkswagen. Additionally, it has emerged as a leader in energy storage solutions (ESS) that improve the efficiency and reliability of renewable energy for the power grid. CATL has signed several large ESS deals, including in China where authorities are targeting a significant increase in energy storage capacity by 2027. CATL has continued to generate significant free cash flow, which it is reinvesting in technology and returning to shareholders through stock buybacks.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM)(TSMC) was another notable contributor. As one of the world's dominant logic chip foundries, TSMC is a leading supplier of graphic processing units for companies, such as NVIDIA, that are at the forefront of the AI buildout. Excitement around the AI supply chain grew as leading hyperscalers and other technology companies continued to announce substantive investments in high-performance computing. We believe TSMC is well positioned to capitalize on this spending due to its unparalleled scale, deep technological advantages, and partnerships with major fabless semiconductor companies and hyperscalers. The company has further expanded its market leadership as competitors have stumbled or shifted away from the foundry business, leaving TSMC as the unparalleled market leader in this space. And yet, TSMC continues to trade at an attractive valuation discount to its customers.

Manager outlook

We have been reassured by the continued growth in the global economy, and we have been pleased to see many of our companies make progress on their growth trajectories. While we acknowledge economic headwinds, we believe interest rate cuts in the U.S. and other markets could help spur increased global economic activity. We continue to find compelling investment opportunities across sectors and geographies, and we remain excited about innovations we have seen in areas such as information technology, healthcare, and energy production and storage. At the same time, we caution that valuations in some pockets of the market appear increasingly disconnected from underlying fundamentals.

In this environment, we believe active management is critical to managing risk. We remain committed to sifting through opportunities to identify companies positioned for superior free-cash-flow growth that we believe is undervalued by the market. Our focus remains on companies with exceptional management teams, high-quality balance sheets, strong competitive positioning, and high or improving returns on invested capital. We have taken steps to cushion the portfolio against potential market volatility by investing in companies with recurring revenues and earnings visibility that we believe can outperform across market environments. We view this disciplined, fundamentals-driven approach as key to delivering long-term capital appreciation for our investors.

Returns quoted are past performance and do not guarantee future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Investment returns and principal value will vary; there may be a gain or loss when shares are sold. For the most recent month-end performance call 800.668.0434 or visit janushenderson.com/performance.

Definitions

Monetary policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money. Return On Invested Capital (ROIC) is a measure of how effectively a company used the money invested in its operations.

Free-cash-flow (FCF) yield is a financial ratio that measures how much cash flow a company has in case of its liquidation or other obligations by comparing the free cash flow per share with the market price per share and indicates the level of cash flow the company will earn against its share market value.

Please consider the charges, risks, expenses and investment objectives carefully before investing. For a prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus containing this and other information, please call Janus Henderson at 800.668.0434 or download the file from janushenderson.com/info. Read it carefully before you invest or send money.

Returns include reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Discussion is based on the performance of Class I Shares.

The opinions are as of 09/30/25, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Mutual fund performance for some periods may incorporate returns from older share classes, adjusted for fees and expenses. Visit janushenderson.com/synthetic for specific details. Holdings are subject to change without notice.

For equity portfolios, relative contribution compares the performance of a security in the portfolio to the benchmark’s total return, factoring in the difference in weight of that security in the benchmark. Returns are calculated using daily returns and previous day ending weights rolled up by ticker, gross of advisory fees, may exclude certain derivatives and does not represent actual performance.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

Foreign securities are subject to additional risks including currency fluctuations, political and economic uncertainty, increased volatility, lower liquidity and differing financial and information reporting standards, all of which are magnified in emerging markets.

Equity securities are subject to risks including market risk. Returns will fluctuate in response to issuer, political and economic developments.

ESG Integration Risk. There is a risk that considering ESG Factors as part of the Fund’s investment process may fail to produce the intended results or that the Fund may perform differently from funds that have a similar investment style but do not formally incorporate such considerations in their strategy. Information related to ESG Factors provided by issuers and third parties, which portfolio management may utilize, continues to develop, and may be incomplete or inaccurate, use different methodologies, or be applied differently across issuers and industries.

ESG Integration. As part of its investment process, portfolio management considers ESG risks and opportunities (“ESG Factors”) that it believes are financially material, alongside other fundamental investment factors. Examples of potential financially material ESG Factors include corporate governance, company culture, exposure to climate change, and human capital management. To assess ESG Factors, portfolio management uses issuer reports, third-party data, and internally-generated analyses and may engage directly with issuers. ESG Factors are one of many considerations in the investment decision-making process and may not be determinative in deciding to include or exclude an investment from the portfolio.

Smaller capitalization securities may be less stable and more susceptible to adverse developments, and may be more volatile and less liquid than larger capitalization securities. Actively managed investment portfolios are subject to the risk that the investment strategies and research process employed may fail to produce the intended results. Accordingly, a portfolio may underperform its benchmark index or other investment products with similar investment objectives.

data.title: Aeluma: Revenue Growth Meets Heavy Spending, Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction And Investment Thesis

I am downgrading Aeluma, Inc. (ALMU) to a Hold from my previous Buy rating, driven by the operational spending, such as manufacturing readiness and commercialization execution. I am still optimistic about Aeluma on a long-term view, looking at the latest (Q1 2026) spike in top-line growth, which rose from $481,000 to $1.4 million, representing a 191% increase YoY. Honestly, even though the stock is trending downwards, it has a promising future if you look at its spending. I find it aligned with the growth of the semiconductor industry, which I highlighted in my previous article. For example, as new contracts keep coming in, this suggests that the company is heading in the right direction.

However, in what I can consider to be a blessing in disguise, Aeluma has to pay a price to find solid ground for a sustained bullish reversal. The company is still investing more in operations like manufacturing equipment to execute the contracts I highlighted in my previous article. But for an investor, I am cautious because right now volume orders are yet to be secured, and once secured, the company may take 6 months to 12 months to stand up its outside capacity. This means that the current increased operational spending may not see a recoup of the value not later than 6 months to 12 months, which is when I expect a solid bullish reversal.

So far, the company’s stock price has plummeted by 19%, underperforming the S&P 500 since my previous article. With that said, I will delve deeper into the root of this to unravel why I think, as it stands now, this company warrants a hold.

Background Of High Operations Spending

Before I get to operations, I want to give you the context of high expenditure, which I think is the commitment to actions towards delivering the contract obligations. In my previous article, I highlighted that this company’s optimism is driven by how contracts keep coming in. I still believe in this perspective, as you can see, the contracts are still coming in, with the latest contract from NASA. In this contract, Aeluma is expected to deliver advanced Quantum Dot Photonics Integrated Circuits for AI applications and aerospace to NASA.

In this article, I have introduced that Aeluma estimates to stand up its outside capacity in around 6 to 12 months, which is within the expected time when it ramps up production to target markets from volume orders. When I dig deeper, I see that Aeluma joining the Midwest Microelectronics Consortium (MMEC) to speed up its pathway to commercial and defense markets is within this outlook. From these operations above, it leads me to understand why operating expenses skyrocketed in Q1 2026 to $2.29 million, representing a 33.92%.

Still, connecting the dots, when I look at the 6-12 months’ timeline when Aeluma is expected to stand up its outside capacity, it relates to the FY2026 guidance where the company seeks to expand its team by hiring more staff and leadership team. The company is looking forward to increasing its validation and test capabilities. There are no exact expenditure estimates, but from the rising expenses and the projected weaker EPS in the coming years, such as a -1,750% EPS growth in 2026, I believe the company is likely to keep investing.

While the immediate effect of the high investment and expenses is weaker profitability, I think we need patience to see if the investment pays off in the long term.

Financials And Earnings Highlights

If there is one area about this company that pleases me, is its revenue performance. Well, the company closed its FY2025 in June this year with a revenue of $4.7 million, marking a YoY growth of 407.6%.

Its trailing revenue stands at $5.6 million, which is a YoY growth rate of 307.2%. More interestingly, the company has maintained at least a triple-digit revenue growth for the last five consecutive quarters, which signals its strong revenue momentum. In my view, this solid topline growth is a testament to a strong demand for its solutions, which I believe justifies the company’s expansion plan. With persistent efforts by the company, such as getting into a consortium to access the market, I expect revenues to keep rising in the coming years. However, I don’t expect the company to sustain its triple-digit revenue growth in the long term due to market saturation. I believe over time it may fall to a strong double-digit growth, which I think is also attractive.

Most importantly, this being a loss-making company, it is important to evaluate the company’s cash runway. With a cash of about $38 million and a trailing cash from operations of about -$1 million, it shows that this company has strong liquidity to sustain its current cash burn, and this shows that the company is on strong financial footing to execute its investment plan.

Moving to earnings, the weight of developing and scaling its manufacturing is felt here. You note that the company’s EPS for Q2 2026 is estimated to hit negative $0.05, and this is expected to bottom further to negative $0.06 until Q1 2027.

Now, the picture of a 6-12 month timeline can be seen closely because this EPS decline continues to June 2027, which is within that range. In June 2027, EPS is estimated to bottom at negative $0.25, representing -32.43%, revealing that the company is likely to experience higher expenses, which its rising revenue can’t offset, leading to lower profitability. This is the basis of my hold rating because until its investment shows a material impact on the bottom line, I expect volatility in the stock.

Investment Take

The company’s momentum reflects this operational spending, which has continued to drive the company’s earnings deeper. Also, I believe the company's continued public offering, for example, with the latest, which raised $25.4 million in gross proceeds, has impacted the stock direction. So far, the company has diluted its outstanding shares from 13 million to 14 million to fund its transition to commercialization, which is becoming a capital-intensive process.

These aspects have deteriorated the company’s stock performance from a 9-month price return of 94.31% to a 3-month price return of -34.53%, which is currently below the sector’s average (-2.81%), a significant difference.

Even further, the relative strength shows that the Aeluma price is in the bearish zone (below 50) and trending downwards. At 36.7, the RSI is yet to hit the oversold region of 20 and as such, with the current EPS weakness and rising expenses, I expect the current downtrend to continue in the mid and short terms. This further forms the basis for my hold recommendation.

Investment Risks

Execution Risk

If you look at the R&D, it also spiked from $0.15 million in Q4 2025 to $0.61 million in Q1 2026, representing a 306.7% QoQ increase. This shows that the company’s capital towards manufacturing preparedness is rising. As such, any missteps in the execution of the planned expansion could see weaker market sentiments sustaining a long term bearish momentum. The company is transitioning from a lab-scale operation to a commercial output in a period of around 6-12 months, which demands additional talent, validation protocols, and new equipment.

As such, I think that delays or unforeseen challenges are likely to prolong this period, and investors may continue experiencing deepening earnings. The wafer-scale processing and backend packaging are complex processes that may need time to achieve the required qualification. Even though it has ample cash, a delay in commercialization may threaten its cash runway, as this is a capital-intensive venture.

Industry Qualification Bottleneck Risk

The go-to-market strategy of Aeluma hinges on the qualification of its semiconductor for quantum computing, aerospace, and defense. Since these sectors impose rigorous standards, this could result in long lead times. Failure to meet these qualifications risks prolonged commercialization outside the projected window. This may continue stalling revenue generation and weaken its promising high-growth momentum.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my hold stance because, despite the company showing high-growth potential, it is currently at an intensive capital injection point towards commercialization. The focus is right now on shifting from lab-scale to commercial operations, which is why I think it warrants a hold rating.

data.title: Waste Management: A Defensive Core Holding With Underappreciated Growth Power
data.description:
Although Waste Management, Inc. (WM) looks like a defensive, boring, and slow-moving stock to be invested in when growth markets are going strong, it has its merits even in a growth portfolio. The defensive credentials are true and may walk into any portfolio on its recession-resistant, even AI-resistant appeal (portfolio stabilizer). However, the Buy thesis does not stop at that. Despite rallies in the past couple of years, valuations haven't exploded (more of a rally in line with fundamentals). So, the fear of heights and questions around whether it is too late to invest in WM look like a minor concern.

I see enough room for continued growth, even if the ESG optionalities do not lead to explosive growth in the future. And just that level of continued incremental growth and improvements has seen WM perform very well (beyond the stable expectations) many times in the past. In fact, I see a lot of scope for capital appreciation (in addition to a stable dividend) over a long-term horizon of 2-3 years, particularly starting from where we are now.

What We Know About Waste Management

WM's business model is centered around collection, transfer, recycling, and disposal services—inherently defensive. Waste generation is essential and recurring, giving the company predictable revenue even during economic slowdowns. Long-term contracts, regional scale, and a regulatory framework that limits competition and further strengthens its defensive appeal.

This stability is very visible in its financials. The TTM revenue graph hardly shows any material contraction through 2008 and 2020. In fact, revenue growth has accelerated in the past 5 years. Even the TTM revenues today show a ~12% increase against the full year 2024 numbers. WM’s revenue growth has been driven by long-term supportive factors like population growth and urbanization. However, in the past 5 years, the re-acceleration in topline is additionally due to several evolving factors: aggressive pricing, Advanced Disposal (AD) acquisition (adding customers and routes inorganically), recycled commodity price increases (cardboard, plastics, and metal rose sharply in 2021-22), and new sustainability initiatives.

WM's utility like FCF and EBITDA margin stability and overall growing trajectory also supports its strong defensive thesis. EBITDA margins in particular are showing stable increasing trends in the past 5 years. FCF has been a little more volatile due to sustainability investments but still remains predictable and growing overall.

Valuations Are Still Reasonable

WM's EV to forward EBITDA is now at ~14.4x, at average levels compared to the 3-year data visible in the charts below. Even the 5-year average is almost exactly what the ratio looks like today. So, historically, valuations are not looking expensive. All the ~80% gains in WM share prices are therefore attributed to real topline and earnings improvements rather than a rerating story.

In fact, EBITDA margins have only moved higher post-COVID and remain at reasonably strong levels despite recent marginal contractions. The concerning metric is a decline in FCF yields despite rising revenue and a healthy EBITDA—FCF yield has compressed meaningfully over the past decade—from ~4% (pre- and immediately post-COVID) to roughly 2.1% today. However, this decline is not the result of operational weakness. WM has invested in fleet modernization, renewable natural gas projects, and recycling upgrades. This is supportive of its pricing power (as seen in topline growth) and long-term efficiencies. Effects of any AD acquisition or working capital swings are not structural.

According to management commentary in the Q3 2025 earnings call, the easing of capex should lift FCF, which has already been improving in the past couple of quarters.

Notably, our operating EBITDA to free cash flow conversion approached 42% in the third quarter, reflecting that we have moved from peak investment levels in sustainability growth projects, landfill infrastructure, and our fleet into a period where we will harvest strong returns on these investments.

Looking ahead, along with FCF improvements, core price improvements of 6% look well above inflation. WM emphasized that price realization and contract repricing remain strong across commercial and industrial customer segments. Q3 growth in revenues was in the mid-teens, and even industrial demand picked up after years of stagnation. Management also emphasized margin improvements to offset any demand softness (guidance has upgraded EBITDA margins).

I think that what you're left with is recognizing that with softness on the revenue line, there had to be outperformance in our execution, particularly in the collection and disposal business on margin, and that's what lifts our confidence in the top end of that range.

Outlook Bullish—Use Long-Term Lens

WM’s revenue guidance for FY2025 was moved to the low end of the prior range, driven entirely by softer recycled commodity prices and modest weakness in WM Healthcare Solutions. Importantly, this adjustment does not reflect deterioration in WM’s core business. Pricing remains strong, volumes are broadly stable, and the collection–landfill engine continues to deliver margin expansion. The revenue softness is centered in low-margin categories, so the EBITDA and FCF impact is limited. In fact, WM raised its free cash flow outlook as the company moves past peak investment levels, confirming that underlying cash generation is improving even as headline revenue fluctuates. Guidance projects FCF for the full year to be ~$2.85b at the midpoint, continuing improvements that could push the FCF yield towards ~2.5%.

Overall, as markets have retraced WM share prices on this weaker guidance, I feel the opportunity for fresh entries becomes even stronger now. As RNG projects contribute further cash and capex normalizes, the FCF rebound looks good. Revenue softness is not across the board and there is additional support coming in from sustainable (slow ramp, but multi-year revenue visibility).

Since I view WM from a long-term lens, any short-term pressure due to recycling commodity prices or retraction in industrial volumes does not make the long-term Buy thesis any weaker. I still see high-double digit growth in 2026 even if demand remains soft—levels that have supported similar valuations in the past for WM (2023 revenue growth was in fact in lower single digits with similar valuations at play). This expectation of high-single digit growth is in line with the implied ~8.5% revenue growth guided for Q4 2025 (derived from full-year guidance minus realized revenues in the past 3 quarters).

Lessons From History

WM's defensive case is well known and appreciated. What is less appreciated is its growth-like total return potential. This is not just a future story but has been seen multiple times in the past. In the 3-year price returns chart below, we can see the average 3-year return mark has often been in the 40-60% range. Even 80% returns are not a rarity—this propels its positioning almost into a growth trajectory.

Interestingly, the minimum 3-year return has never been below ~20%. Add a stable yield of ~1.5% (growing for the past 21 years, last 5-year growth of ~8.5% itself beating inflation comfortably), and investors have rarely reaped anything less than 7-8% over a 3-year horizon. The combination of defensiveness, share price increasing on fundamentals over narratives (no valuation rerating burden), and possibility of 20-25% CAGR returns over 3 years makes a compelling case for WM.

The critical aspect of this outlook is that the 3-year return data has not seen unusual valuation bubble-led returns or required explosive growth or margin expansions. For WM, steady has done it more often than not. Now we have a revenue softness that is nowhere near historical lows. On the other hand, we have a better thesis on margins and cash flow. Interestingly, the current 3-year return is only ~29%, towards the lower end of WM's historical returns. Investments now are late—they are timing it quite close to the best entry point for a long-term horizon.

WM becomes a Strong Buy because of the macro conditions as well, when economic uncertainties and AI rallies require a strong defense in the portfolio. WM is that stabilizer that assures decent returns with the possibility of significant upside, too. For investors wary of short-term dynamics, I recommend systematic accumulation over the next couple of quarters. I am looking from a long-term perspective and do see a good opportunity now overall. I have purposefully taken longer time frame charts to drive home the story of where WM is relative to history and how history has waded through worse with great results. I do not see any concerns around short-term drags (if any) and timing fresh entries.

data.title: There's A Bigger Threat To Markets Unfolding Now, And It's Not AI Infrastructure
data.description:
Introduction

Over the last few weeks, there’s been a decent degree of anxiety in the market. While it seems like the selling pressure has taken a holiday this week, investors appear to be expressing anxiety over a possible bubble in AI infrastructure, led by OpenAI’s (OPENAI) $1.4 trillion in capital/financial commitments. While the AI infrastructure building is an issue worth watching, there’s another issue unfolding right now that investors should be aware of, which is liquidity.

What Is Liquidity?

Liquidity is how much cash is available on hand. In our economy, we are looking at it as uncommitted cash, money that has nowhere to go, so it ends up at the Federal Reserve. This is distinctly different than the cash sitting on a corporation’s balance sheet, because even that cash is put to work as deposits in the banking system that are lent out.

So how do we measure excess cash? The Federal Reserve uses overnight reverse repurchase agreements to measure excess cash in the economy. The metric is measured each business day. Simply described, firms can go to the Federal Reserve with their excess cash and buy Treasury bonds for interest on an overnight basis.

Back in 2022, the amount of excess cash coming to the Fed exceeded $2 trillion, a clear signal that there was too much money in the financial system. Too much money chasing too few goods creates inflation, which we have experienced at above 2% for more than three years. The Federal Reserve raised interest rates and engaged in quantitative tightening (the selling of US Treasuries) to pull that excess cash out of the economy, and they have succeeded on that front. Excess cash at the Fed totaled $2.2 billion on Wednesday, a 1,000-fold decrease.

A Built-In Circuit Breaker

The Federal Reserve does have a facility to handle when excess cash becomes too low, and it works in the opposite way to what I previously described. In this case, the Federal Reserve will purchase Treasury securities from firms that need to have cash on hand, and they do this on an overnight basis as well. This facility remained relatively quiet until last month, when firms required $29 billion on October 31st to meet month-end obligations.

While the circuit breaker of providing liquidity sounds like a good offset, it is only temporary. Firms need Treasury securities to participate, and the act of exchanging Treasuries for cash with the Fed is not free, as there is an interest expense associated with it. If the Federal Reserve is engaged in operations to lend overnight, it signals that firms are under stress for cash and that stress will eventually broaden throughout the economy.

Zooming In - A Look At The Last 30 Days And Net Liquidity

Since the Federal Reserve monitors this activity each business day, it’s important to zoom in on the last month to get a detailed view of the situation as it develops. Since November 7th, the Federal Reserve has sold less than $10 billion in Treasuries per day (the measure of excess cash), and while the purchase of Treasuries by the Fed (cash injections) has been small, it has occurred for two straight days and will likely spike again at month's end to meet obligations.

So, what’s the best way to quantify the two indicators together? I’ve been taking Treasuries sold (excess cash) and deducting Treasuries purchased (cash injections) and creating a metric I’ve called net liquidity. It measures the net of excess cash available in the economy after accounting for overnight borrowing from the Fed. On November 17th, net liquidity was negative for the first time. On November 25th and 26th, it was negative for two consecutive days.

What Does Negative Net Liquidity Look Like, And What is the Fed Doing About It?

It’s hard to get into the specifics about exactly what will transpire if current conditions continue or worsen, but I have a general idea. Essentially, we will have gone from an economy where too much money was chasing too few assets to an economy where too little money is pursuing too many assets.

When assets aren’t in the level of demand that they used to be, the market adjusts by lowering prices to make them more attractive. This concept is very generic in nature and will likely lead to distortions, as areas where demand is outstripping supply will likely continue to experience price increases, but in theory, there should be an offset in another industry because the economy simply does not possess the cash to support the level of pricing for all goods and services. This could lead to higher interest rates (lower debt prices), changes in consumer goods and services, and/or changes in asset prices ranging from stocks to real estate.

The Federal Reserve has already made a decision that may support the liquidity situation. In their last meeting, the open market committee voted to end quantitative tightening on December 1st. This would stop the current $5 billion per month that the Federal Reserve is selling Treasuries to the open market (and taking in cash). The move will support liquidity by $5 billion per month or $1.25 billion per week. The daily monitoring of overnight reverse repurchase agreements now becomes important, leading up to the December meeting because it will determine if stability returns or if the Fed will need to take further action.

Portfolio Positioning & Conclusion

While the idea of asset and debt prices falling would lead people to go to cash, I’m not quick to make any radical changes and currently have a decent cash stash designated for volatility. Instead, I’m positioning myself for what would follow. I do not believe that the Federal Reserve will tolerate volatility for any extended time; therefore, I believe we’ll return to quantitative easing in 2026.

With the return of QE, I’m particularly curious to see how my fixed income and preferred share positions behave, as lower interest rates should result in higher prices. Having a good cash position can help add here if prices drop too far and create attractive yields, or maybe stocks sell off and create value opportunities. Either way, having the flexibility of some cash through what comes next will be important for investors.

data.title: Thornburg Summit Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Global equity markets extended their rally through the third quarter, buoyed by renewed optimism over easing monetary policy, solid corporate earnings, and resilient investor sentiment. The Federal Reserve delivered its long-anticipated rate cut in September, lowering rates by 25 basis points in response to signs of a cooling labor market and broader economic moderation. In contrast, the Bank of Japan continued to lift its yield-curve control by gradually scaling back its asset-purchases, letting the market exert more influence on its sovereign yields. After easing monetary policy since the spring of last year, the European Central Bank kept its key rate flat this summer, as a rebound in Eurozone growth slowed and inflation ebbed toward target. China's central bank provided more liquidity to counter weak demand and support credit supply.

Trade tensions remain a significant theme, with continuing negotiations between the U.S. and its major trade partners, including China, which is also in contentious trade talks and tit-for-tat tariff and procurement walls with the European Union. But technology-related industries showed strength, particularly in Northeast Asia and the U.S., where chip stocks rallied on AI optimism.

Treasury yields fluctuated throughout the period, with short-end yields declining approximately 30-40 basis points as rate-cut expectations increased. The curve steepened with the 30-year yield falling 7 basis points since the start of the quarter. Despite weakening labor market indicators, including the narrowest labor market differential since early 2021 in the September Consumer Confidence survey, economic data remained positive, supporting narratives of consumer resilience, improved trade clarity, and stronger corporate capital expenditure. As the quarter ended, the federal government faced an imminent shutdown with no funding deal reached by September 30.

Looking ahead, potential bouts of heightened volatility could present opportunities for active managers.

Third Quarter 2025 Performance Highlights

  • In 3Q25 the portfolio (I share class) returned 6.66%, 188 basis points ahead of the Blended Index (60% MSCI ACWI Index and 40% Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index). Year to date the portfolio (I share class) returned 16.09%, 185 basis points ahead of the Blended Index.
  • On a sector basis, stock selection within communication services and information technology contributed positively to relative results vs the MSCI ACWI Index. Stock selection to health care and energy detracted from relative performance.
  • From a geographic perspective, stock selection within UK and Korea contributed positively to relative results. Stock selection within Japan and Brazil detracted from relative performance.
  • The portfolio's bond exposure outperformed the fixed income portion of the blended index. Yield curve positioning, as well as an over allocation to U.S. dollar denominated bonds, were tailwinds during the quarter.

Current Positioning and Outlook

Our portfolio positioning entering the quarter, which included an underweight to U.S. equities, continued to reflect caution about historically high valuations relative to many segments of international markets. While our allocation to equities during the quarter was only modestly higher than the index, our bias towards non-U.S. equities was a tailwind. Entering the last quarter of the year, we continue to believe that large valuation gaps between the U.S. and the rest of the world present potential for relative value and diversification benefits, and while we are not making a macro call on specific regions, we believe the international landscape offers compelling opportunities for stock pickers.

Within fixed income, higher bond yields are presenting a better setup for fixed income, but credit spreads remain tighter than their historical averages. While we continue to take a defensive posture both from a rates and credit perspective, we are taking advantage of the higher income opportunities that bonds are now offering the portfolio.

Markets appear increasingly desensitized to geopolitical events—a trend that began around the onset of the Russia-Ukraine war. We view this complacency as dangerous, as the eventual tipping point could trigger an outsized reaction. As such, looking ahead, potential bouts of heightened volatility could present opportunities for active managers.

Thornburg Summit Fund (TSUMX)

Portfolio Managers

Neal BasuMullick, CFAPortfolio Manager

Lon Erickson, CFAPortfolio Manager

Supported by the entire Thornburg investment team

data.title: FS Specialty Lending Fund: Massive Price To NAV Discount Likely To Close Soon
data.description:
Introduction and Listing Circumstances

FS Specialty Lending Fund (OTCPK:FSSL) is a senior secured loan fund. It had previously operated as a closed-end fund but recently became listed on the stock market. In the past, FSSL had sold shares above the NAV, which led some investors to feel frustrated with subpar returns. This frustration reached a peak when Cox Capital Partners made a tender offer to FSSL shareholders for $11.50 per share, well below the then-estimated NAV of $19.82. FSSL quickly advised shareholders to vote no, and then shortly thereafter, FSSL was listed on the market, with the hope that this would allow shareholders to sell at a much more attractive price.

It is a key part of my thesis to understand that the uplisting was not done to grow the fund by attracting new capital, as is commonly done with an IPO. No new shares were issued, and no capital was raised by the fund, which the company itself shows on their own presentation:

Rather, this was specifically designed as a "liquidity event," with the goal of allowing shareholders the ability to get out of the fund at more attractive prices than the Cox tender offer. Therefore, it should be no surprise that trading opened below the fund's NAV and then continued to trade downwards.

NAV Discount Creates Opportunity

FSSL currently trades at over a 28% discount to NAV, with a 12.57% dividend yield. This is a bit unusual. The two most actively traded senior loan funds, Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) and State Street® Blackstone Senior Loan ETF (SRLN), effectively trade at a discount of zero. In fact, all the top 7 most active senior loan funds trade at a discount of less than one percent.

Still, a large disconnect in senior loan funds has been known to occur occasionally. These situations tend to happen when numerous risk factors align: it may be a difficult time for funds, the fund may have a disliked manager, perceived risky holdings, a poor reputation of the firm, high fees, or excessive use of leverage. When this happens, you can sometimes see a discount of 10-15% to NAV for these bottom-tier players.

I am no starry-eyed optimist when it comes to FSSL—I do believe that most of the boxes above are checked for this fund, and thus I believe a fair value for the fund is likely around 10-15% below NAV. Even the fund itself has alluded to this lower valuation level being something of a goal, prominently featuring this chart in its investor materials:

As you can see from the above chart, some funds tend to begin trading at a very large discount to NAV, with the discount tending to close at an average of 12% below NAV after roughly half a year. Even if this process took a full year, at today's prices, that would be roughly a 12.5% return in share appreciation on top of a 12.5% dividend yield, for a 25% total return. These numbers would be even higher if we were starting with FSSL's 28% discount. Meanwhile, the above-mentioned BKLN is yielding about 6.5%. So even if FSSL merely rises to the valuation level of an average bottom-tier fund, the potential for outperformance could be huge.

Risks are Priced In

There are a few risks involved for FSSL, but I believe the massive discount to NAV more than compensates for these risks. First, FSSL is currently leveraged at 1.26x, which does amplify losses in downturns and creates higher fees for the fund, which total a little over 5% per year. While higher risk and high fees are unappealing, they are also a large part of what allows it to outperform "safer" senior loan funds like BKLN.

The second risk is the asset quality itself. While the majority of the debt is senior secured first lien at 78%, the remaining 22% dabbles in the riskier parts of the credit space:

While private lending loans do carry risk, the risk is often overstated. For example, in the most recent period measured, roughly 1.76% of private credit loans were in default. Meanwhile, FSSL's record was even better than that: only 0.9% of loans on the books were in default on an amortized cost basis. Meanwhile, even during once-in-a-generation blowups like the 2008 financial crisis, the recovery rate among senior loans was 70%. Measured against these figures, in my opinion, the 28% discount to NAV looks too big relative to the relatively small risk.

Lastly, the loans in the fund are relatively diverse by sector, so there's no additional risk there:

The Bottom Line

FSSL's unique listing situation is likely the cause of the massive undervaluation. The fund's slightly less than pristine asset pool, disliked fund manager, high fees, and use of leverage are also contributing factors in making the fund a bottom-tier player, and I predict it will face a persistent discount to NAV for some time. However, even rising to the level of the average bottom-tier fund would be a massive gain for FSSL, and meanwhile, the extra risk is more than compensated by a yield that is nearly double that of other senior loan funds. With less than 1% of loans in default, there's no reason to lose sleep on this one. I rate FSSL a buy, with a price target of $16.40, or a price-to-NAV discount of 12.5%

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
